questions.text: A projectile is launched at point O and follows the path OPQRS, as shown, Air resistance may be neglected. Which statement is true for the projectile when it is at the highest point Q of its path?
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: The horizontal component of the projectiles acceleration is zero
options.explanation: This is true. The horizontal component of acceleration is zero at heighest point during projectile motion, and throughout the motion.

options.text: The kinetic energy of the projectile is zero
options.explanation: Kinetic energy is not zero at the highest point during projectile motion. The velocity of the projectile at any point is Vx + Vy = V. Since the horizontal velocity will remain conserved throughout the journey as Ucosθ, V can not be zero. Hence, kinetic energy can not be zero.

options.text: The horizontal component of the projectile's velocity is zero
options.explanation: This statement is erroneous. The horizontal velocity will remain conserved througout the journey as Ucosθ.

options.text: The momentum of the projectile is zero
options.explanation: Momentum is not zero at any point during projectile motion. The velocity of the projectile at any point is vx + vy = v. Since the horizontal velocity will remain conserved throughout the journey as vcosθ, v can not be zero. Hence, momentum can not be zero.

questions.explanation: The horizontal component of the projectile's acceleration is zero at point Q.

questions.text: Angle between action and reaction forces is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The angle between action and reaction forces is not 0 degrees but π radians, or 180 degrees. This is because the action force and reaction force are always opposite in direction and collinear.

The angle between action and reaction forces is π rad, not π/2 rad. This is because action and reaction forces are always in opposite directions and act along the same line. The angle between them is therefore 180 degrees, which is π rad.

The angle between action and reaction forces is 180 degrees or π rad. This is because action and reaction forces are always opposite in direction and collinear. For example, when you push on a wall, the wall pushes back on you with an equal and opposite force. The two forces are in the same line, but they are in opposite directions. The angle between them is therefore 180 degrees. Newton's third law of motion states that for every action there is an equal and opposite reaction. This law is often used to explain why objects move the way they do. For example, when you throw a ball, the ball exerts a force on your hand. This force is the action force. The reaction force is the force that the ball exerts on the air. The reaction force causes the ball to accelerate forward.

The angle between action and reaction forces is 180 degrees, not 2pi radians. This is because action and reaction forces are always opposite in direction and collinear. A line segment is considered to be 180 degrees in angle, not 2pi radians. Radians are a unit of angular measure, while degrees are a unit of angular measure that is based on a circle divided into 360 degrees.

questions.explanation: Option C is correct.The angle between action and reaction forces is 180 degrees or π rad. This is because action and reaction forces are always opposite in direction and collinear. For example, when you push on a wall, the wall pushes back on you with an equal and opposite force. The two forces are in the same line, but they are in opposite directions. The angle between them is therefore 180 degrees. Newton's third law of motion states that for every action there is an equal and opposite reaction. This law is often used to explain why objects move the way they do. For example, when you throw a ball, the ball exerts a force on your hand. This force is the action force. The reaction force is the force that the ball exerts on the air. The reaction force causes the ball to accelerate forward.

questions.text: The P.E gained by projectile when it reaches at maximum height is expressed by equation;
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Please prefer main explanation box.
Please prefer main explanation box.

The potential energy (PE) of a projectile at its maximum height is equal to its kinetic energy (KE) at the point of projection. This is because the projectile has no vertical velocity at its maximum height, so all of its energy is in the form of potential energy. The equation for PE is: PE = mgh where: m is the mass of the projectile g is the acceleration due to gravity h is the height of the projectile The equation for KE is: KE = 1/2mv² where: m is the mass of the projectile v is the velocity of the projectile At the point of projection, the velocity of the projectile is v = vo, where vo is the initial velocity of the projectile. Substituting these equations into the equation for PE, we get: PE = mgh = 1/2mvo² Solving for h, we get: h = 1/2vo²/g The maximum height of the projectile is reached when the vertical component of its velocity is zero. This happens when the projectile has reached its maximum height and is momentarily at rest. The vertical component of the velocity of the projectile is given by: v = vo sin θ where: θ is the angle of projection Setting v = 0 and solving for θ, we get: θ = sin⁻¹(0/vo) The angle of projection at which the projectile reaches its maximum height is always 90 degrees. Therefore, the PE gained by the projectile when it reaches its maximum height is: PE = 1/2vo²/g This is equal to K.E. sin²θ, where: KE is the initial kinetic energy of the projectile θ is the angle of projection Therefore, the answer is true.
Please prefer main explanation box.

Substituting these equations into the equation for PE, we get: PE = mgh = 1/2mvo² Solving for h, we get: h = 1/2vo²/g The maximum height of the projectile is reached when the vertical component of its velocity is zero. Therefore, the PE gained by the projectile when it reaches its maximum height is: PE = 1/2vo²/g This is equal to K.E. sin²θ, where: KE is the initial kinetic energy of the projectile θ is the angle of projection Therefore, the answer is true.

questions.text: A ball is projected horizontally from the top of a cliff on the surface of the earth with a speed of 40 ms-1. Assuming that there is no air resistance, what will be its speed after 3s?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Please prefer main explanation box.
Please prefer main explanation box.

The horizontal velocity of the ball will be the same 3 seconds later. This is because there is no force acting on the ball in the horizontal direction. The vertical velocity of the ball will increase by 30 m/s after 3 seconds. This is because the acceleration due to gravity is 10 m/s² and the ball has been falling for 3 seconds. The speed of the ball 3 seconds later will be: Horizontal Velocity = 40m/s Vertical Velocity = 30m/s Speed = Horizontal Velocity + Vertical Velocity = 40 m/s + 30 m/s = 70 m/s Therefore, the speed of the ball will be 70 m/s 3 seconds later.
Please prefer main explanation box.

questions.explanation: Option C is correct.The horizontal velocity of the ball will be the same 3 seconds later. This is because there is no force acting on the ball in the horizontal direction. The vertical velocity of the ball will increase by 30 m/s after 3 seconds. This is because the acceleration due to gravity is 10 m/s² and the ball has been falling for 3 seconds. The speed of the ball 3 seconds later will be: Horizontal Velocity = 40m/sVertical Velocity = 30m/s Speed = Horizontal Velocity + Vertical Velocity = 40 m/s + 30 m/s = 70 m/s Therefore, the speed of the ball will be 70 m/s 3 seconds later.

questions.text: A projectile is thrown horizontally from a 490m high cliff with a velocity of 100 ms-1. The time taken by projectile to reach the ground is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Incorrect as per the formula
Incorrect as per the formula
Incorrect as per the formula

The following is the solution: We will use the equation: h = (1/2)gt2 where: h = vertical distance fallen (in this case, the height of the cliff = 490 m), g = acceleration due to gravity (approximately 9.8 m/s² on Earth's surface), and t = time taken to reach the ground. We know that the initial vertical velocity (vy) of the projectile is 0 m/s because it is thrown horizontally. The vertical component of the initial velocity is zero since there is no initial upward or downward motion. The vertical distance fallen is the height of the cliff is 490 m. Now, let's solve for time (t): 490 = (1/2) x 9.8 x t2 Divide both sides by (1/2) x 9.8: t2 = 490 / (1/2) x 9.8 t2 = 100 Take the square root of both sides: t = √100 t = 10 seconds So, the time taken by the projectile to reach the ground is 10 seconds. Hence the correct option is D.

questions.explanation: The following is the solution: We will use the equation: h = (1/2)gt2 where: h = vertical distance fallen (in this case, the height of the cliff = 490 m), g = acceleration due to gravity (approximately 9.8 m/s² on Earth's surface), and t = time taken to reach the ground. We know that the initial vertical velocity (vy) of the projectile is 0 m/s because it is thrown horizontally. The vertical component of the initial velocity is zero since there is no initial upward or downward motion. The vertical distance fallen is the height of the cliff is 490 m. Now, let's solve for time (t): 490 = (1/2) x 9.8 x t2 Divide both sides by (1/2) x 9.8: t2 = 490 / (1/2) x 9.8 t2 = 100 Take the square root of both sides: t = √100 t = 10 seconds So, the time taken by the projectile to reach the ground is 10 seconds. Hence the correct option is D.

questions.text: A projectile is launched at 45° to the horizontal with a force of initial kinetic energy "E". Assuming air resistance to be negligible, what will be the kinetic energy of the project when it reaches its highest point?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The following is the solution: When a projectile is launched at an angle of 45 degrees to the horizontal, its horizontal and vertical components of velocity are equal. So, the horizontal velocity (Vx) of the projectile at the highest point is the same as the initial velocity (Vi) multiplied by the cosine of the launch angle: Vx = Vi cos(45 degrees) Since we are considering only the horizontal component of velocity at the highest point, the kinetic energy (K.E-highest) at the highest point can be calculated as follows: K.E-highest = (1/2) x m x Vx2 Substituting the expression for Vx: K.E-highest = (1/2) x m x (Vi cos(45degrees))2 Now, cos(45 degrees) is equal to √2/2: K.E-highest = (1/2) x m x (Vi √2 /2)2 = (1/2) x m x (Vi2 (2 /4)) = (1/2) x m x (Vi2 / 2) Finally, since the initial kinetic energy (Ki) is given as "i," we can write the equation as: K.E-highest = Ki / 2 Therefore, the kinetic energy of the projectile at its highest point will be half of its initial kinetic energy, i.e., K.E-highest = Ki / 2 which can also be written as 0.5 Ki or as the question asked in terms of E, it will become 0.5 E, hence option A is correct.
Incorrect as per the formula
Incorrect as per the formula
Incorrect as per the formula

questions.explanation: The following is the solution: When a projectile is launched at an angle of 45 degrees to the horizontal, its horizontal and vertical components of velocity are equal. So, the horizontal velocity (Vx) of the projectile at the highest point is the same as the initial velocity (Vi) multiplied by the cosine of the launch angle: Vx = Vi cos(45 degrees) Since we are considering only the horizontal component of velocity at the highest point, the kinetic energy (K.E-highest) at the highest point can be calculated as follows: K.E-highest = (1/2) x m x Vx2 Substituting the expression for Vx: K.E-highest = (1/2) x m x (Vi cos(45degrees))2 Now, cos(45 degrees) is equal to √2/2: K.E-highest = (1/2) x m x (Vi √2 /2)2 = (1/2) x m x (Vi2 (2 /4)) = (1/2) x m x (Vi2 / 2) Finally, since the initial kinetic energy (Ki) is given as "i," we can write the equation as: K.E-highest = Ki / 2 Therefore, the kinetic energy of the projectile at its highest point will be half of its initial kinetic energy, i.e., K.E-highest = Ki / 2 which can also be written as 0.5 Ki or as the question asked in terms of E, it will become 0.5 E, hence option A is correct.

questions.text: Two bullets are fired simultaneously, horizontally and with a different speed from the same place. Which bullet will hit the ground first?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: When two bullets are fired horizontally from the same height and at the same time, they both experience the same gravitational acceleration. This means that the force of gravity affects both bullets equally, regardless of their individual speeds or masses. Therefore, both bullets will hit the ground simultaneously. This is one of the fundamental principles of physics, known as the "principle of equivalence" or the "Galilean equivalence principle." It states that in the absence of air resistance, all objects fall at the same rate under the influence of gravity. Therefore, this option is incorrect.

questions.text: In the given figure, a force of 5 N is acting on an object towards East and then it moves towards North under the action of a 6 N force. The resultant force on the object is equal to
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This is incorrect. To determine the resultant force, we need to find the vector sum of the two forces. Adding the magnitudes of the forces (5 N and 6 N) will not give us the correct answer because we need to consider their directions as well.
This is incorrect. Adding the magnitudes of the forces (5 N and 6 N) will give us a total of 11 N. However, this answer does not consider the vector nature of the forces and their directions.

This is the correct answer. To find the resultant force, we need to add the forces vectorially. The 5 N force acting towards the East and the 6 N force acting towards the North are at right angles to each other. We can use the Pythagorean theorem to find the magnitude of the resultant force: Resultant force = √((5 N)² + (6 N)²) = √(25 N² + 36 N²) = √(61 N²) = √61 N.

This is incorrect. As mentioned before, simply adding the magnitudes of the forces will not give us the correct answer. The resultant force depends on both the magnitudes and directions of the forces.

questions.explanation: To find the resultant force, we need to add the forces vectorially. The 5 N force acting towards the East and the 6 N force acting towards the North are at right angles to each other. We can use the Pythagorean theorem to find the magnitude of the resultant force: Resultant force = √((5 N)² + (6 N)²) = √(25 N² + 36 N²) = √(61 N²) = √ 61 N.
